column2: The general practice of cleaning hands with soap-and-water or an alcohol-based hand rub to remove or kill microorganisms before and after patient care.
column4: Hand hygiene is the single most effective measure for preventing the spread of healthcare-associated infections.

column2: A gel, foam, or liquid containing 60–95% alcohol used to decontaminate hands when they are not visibly soiled; also abbreviated ABHR.
column4: An alcohol-based hand rub kills most pathogens in about 20 seconds without a sink.
Most hospital hallways now have an ABHR dispenser outside every patient room.

column2: A hand wash using plain or antimicrobial soap under running water for at least 20 seconds, followed by drying with a paper towel.
column4: A soap-and-water handwash is required when hands are visibly soiled or after caring for a patient with C. difficile.

column2: The WHO framework of five indications for hand hygiene during patient care: before patient contact, before an aseptic task, after body-fluid exposure risk, after patient contact, and after contact with patient surroundings.
column4: The Five Moments tell you when to clean your hands, not how.

column2: A clinical procedure in which microorganisms must be kept out of a normally sterile site, such as inserting an IV or handling a sterile dressing.
column4: Hand hygiene is required immediately before an aseptic task even if you just cleaned your hands at the door.

column2: Any contact with blood, secretions, excretions, mucous membranes, or broken skin that may carry pathogens.
column4: After a body-fluid exposure you clean your hands before touching anything else — even before leaving the bedside.

column2: The patient and the surfaces and equipment immediately around them that become contaminated with the patient's flora during care.
column4: Touching the bedside table counts as contact with the patient zone and triggers a Five-Moments hand hygiene.

column2: An infection a patient acquires during the course of receiving care that was not present on admission.
column4: Missed hand hygiene is the leading preventable cause of a healthcare-associated infection.
Every missed hand-hygiene moment is an opportunity for an HAI.

column2: A dormant, tough-shelled form some bacteria (such as C. difficile) produce that resists alcohol and many disinfectants.
column4: Alcohol-based hand rub does not kill a C. difficile spore — you have to wash the spores off mechanically with soap and water.

column2: The rate at which healthcare workers perform hand hygiene at the indicated moments, usually measured by direct observation.
column4: The Joint Commission expects hospitals to monitor hand-hygiene compliance under National Patient Safety Goal 07.
